ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers fish finger as a food product category; this record is an Italian-style homemade variant with dory fillet and breadcrumb coating, served with potato wedges and tomato sauce.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products, breaded or battered
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers breaded/battered fish products as a category; no specific code exists for Italian-style bastoncini di pesce with dory fillet.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Italian-style breaded fish fingers; SNOMED CT does not have a granular food product code for this preparation.

serving.description: 1 plate (fish fingers with potato wedges and tomato sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of bastoncini di pesce comprises approximately 300 g, including 4–6 breaded dory fish finger pieces, a side of hot potato wedges, and a portion of tomato or spicy tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish fillet, breaded, baked or fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15220 / FDC ID 175168); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for potato wedges (FDC ID 170026) and tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457). Composite estimate weighted 55% breaded fish, 35% potato wedges, 10% tomato sauce.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Italian-style bastoncini di pesce with dory fillet exists in any approved primary source. Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy breaded fish fillet (FDC 175168), potato wedges (FDC 170026), and tomato sauce (FDC 170457), weighted by approximate plate composition: 55% breaded fish, 35% potato wedges, 10% tomato sauce. Values represent the mixed plate per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Fish fingers / fish cakes (breaded, fried) GI ≈ 38–54; midpoint estimate 48 used.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Italian-style bastoncini di pesce with dory fillet. GI estimated at 48 based on breaded fish finger / fish cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The breadcrumb coating and potato wedge component both contribute moderate glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 55.5) / 100 ≈ 26.6, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 18.5 × 3 = 55.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per full plate serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a plate and request low-sodium tomato sauce, as the full serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium — nearly 42% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~26.6 per full plate) driven by breadcrumb coating and potato wedges
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the full plate carries a glycemic load of approximately 26.6, primarily from the breadcrumb coating and potato wedges; reducing the potato wedge portion and pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g) and frying oil contribution; beneficial omega-3 from dory fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While dory fish provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3, the breadcrumb frying process adds saturated and trans fats; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer an oven-baked preparation and limit the full plate to an occasional meal rather than a regular dietary staple.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bastoncini di Pesce (Italian Fish Fingers) is an Italian-style dish consisting of dory fish fillets (pangasius) coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and pan-fried or oven-baked until golden and crisp. As served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore in Singapore, the dish is plated with hot potato wedges and accompanied by tomato sauce or spicy tomato sauce, making it a hearty main course. The exterior is crunchy from the breadcrumb crust while the interior remains moist and flaky. Dory fish is a mild, white-fleshed fish that absorbs seasoning well and is widely used in Italian-inspired preparations. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) from the fish, moderate carbohydrates (~18.5 g per 100 g) from the breadcrumb coating and potato wedges, and moderate fat (~7.2 g per 100 g) from the frying process. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low GI), though the glycemic load per full plate is moderate at approximately 26.6 due to the carbohydrate-dense potato wedge side. Sodium content is notable at approximately 320 mg per 100 g, reaching close to 960 mg per full plate, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The dish contains fish, gluten (wheat breadcrumbs), and egg allergens. It is suitable for halal diets. Clinically, it is a moderate-concern dish for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, glycemic load, and fat profile, though the omega-3 content from dory fish offers some cardiovascular benefit.
common_user_queries: Fish fingers with potato wedges Singapore
Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore fish dish

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Italian-style bastoncini di pesce with dory fillet exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS). Values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for breaded fish fillet, potato wedges, and tomato sauce, weighted by approximate plate composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates (55% breaded dory fish fillet, 35% potato wedges, 10% tomato sauce) derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on breaded fish finger / fish cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.1), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (310.0), calcium_mg (38.0), iron_mg (1.1), cholesterol_mg (38.0), vitamin_c_mg (6.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.12), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.22), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.9), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (1.2), vitamin_e_mg (1.0), phosphorus_mg (165.0), magnesium_mg (24.0), zinc_mg (0.6), selenium_mcg (14.0), and water_g (58.0) are all estimated from USDA composite; no sentinel -1 values are used as reasonable estimates were derivable. 4. food_group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary protein component is dory fish fillet. 5. Multilingual local_names are null for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese as this is an Italian dish served in Singapore; the multilingual naming rules in PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md mandate those languages only for Singaporean dishes, not Italian cuisine. 6. Halal status is marked true as dory fish is permissible and no pork or alcohol ingredients are indicated; this should be confirmed with the restaurant. 7. The input food_group 'Grains and staples' was incorrect and has been corrected.
